macro_segments.title: Introduction and Context: Items Prohibited from Being Worn on Shabbat

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and review of previous discussion on clothing and ornaments
Discussion of the kvul (scarf/cap worn under a hairnet) and slave identification pendants

macro_segments.title: Excursus on Slave Law: Obligations of Jewish Slaves and Non-Jewish Slaves on Shabbat

micro_segments.title: Debate over which slaves are obligated in Shabbat law: the position of the Ravad
Discussion of master's responsibility for slave and servant rest on Shabbat
Question about the relationship between the Gemara's categorical structure and halachic application

macro_segments.title: Slave Wearing Identification Pendants: The Metal vs. Plaster Distinction

micro_segments.title: Samuel's ruling: slave may wear pendant around neck but not on garment
Contradiction with baraita prohibiting pendant even around neck; reconciliation via master's concern for the object's value
Why pendants do not become tamei: neither jewelry nor vessels; likely a form of clothing
The status of pendants: clothing vs. jewelry vs. vessels—an unresolved puzzle
Material distinction: metal pendants create concern if dropped; plaster pendants do not
Statement of Rav Nachman bar Ami: master's concern (makpid) as criterion for permissibility

macro_segments.title: Social Norms and Halachic Categories: Men's Clothing, Women's Clothing, and Tzniut

micro_segments.title: Discussion of how social norms affect halachic categorization of clothing (beged ish, beged isha)
Debate over whether rabbinic identifications of specific items lock in categories even when social reality changes

macro_segments.title: Bells (Zug) on Slaves and Persons: Jewelry vs. Woven Ornaments

micro_segments.title: Slave cannot wear bell around neck; can wear bell woven into garment
Concern that loose bell will break and be carried; woven bells are secure
Principle 'kol she-arag lo gazru': anything woven is not a problem; applied to bells and hair ornaments differently

macro_segments.title: Animal Wearing Identification Tags and Bells: Biblical vs. Rabbinic Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Animal cannot wear identification tag (biblically a burden) or bells
Neither animal tags nor bells become tamei; no concept of jewelry for animals
Contradiction: baraita says bell on animal becomes tamei; reconciliation via knocker (inbal)

macro_segments.title: Retention of Tamei Status: The Sticky Principle and Difficulty of Losing Vessel Status

micro_segments.title: Bell moved from door to animal: becomes tamei despite being affixed to door; once assigned to animal, resists changing status
Vessels enter tamei status easily via machshavah (intention) but exit only via shinui ma'aseh (physical change)

macro_segments.title: The Knocker (Inbal) Distinction: When Does a Bell Become Tamei?

micro_segments.title: Bell with knocker becomes tamei (makes sound, is a vessel); without knocker it does not
Scriptural basis: things that make sound from Parashat Yitro (spoils of Midyan)
Two interpretations: (1) sound-makers always become tamei; (2) Tosafot: person uses them to locate animals
Bells for infants/cribs: only tamei with knocker; bells for adults: tamei even without knocker (ornament)
Analogy: Rav Moshe on wearing a watch without an eiruv—only if you'd wear it even if not working

macro_segments.title: Retention of Tamei Status: The Sticky Principle and Difficulty of Losing Vessel Status (continued)

micro_segments.title: Removal of knocker does not change status because it is easily replaceable (hedyot yakhol lehakhziro)

macro_segments.title: Scissors and Multi-Part Vessels: Chibbur (Connection) for Tumah vs. Haza'ah (Sprinkling)

micro_segments.title: Scissors blades connected for tumah; separate for sprinkling of parah adumah water
Raba's principle: distinction between bish'at melachah (during use) and shelo bish'at melachah (when not in use)
Rabbinical stringency: considered connected for tumah in both cases; separate for haza'ah in both cases

macro_segments.title: Return to Knocker: Rava vs. Abaye and Alternative Uses for Bells Without Knockers

micro_segments.title: Abaye's challenge: if knocker removed, scissors analogy says it becomes two separate vessels
Rava's response: bell can still be used by banging it on pottery; not dependent on knocker alone
Rabbi Yochanan's view: bell could alternatively be used as a water scoop for infants
Unresolved question: does change of function preserve or change vessel status?—to be continued
